coredata.dc:title: Single-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots and applied for dopamine sensing, in vitro multicolor cellular imaging as well as fluorescent ink
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Amit
Asu, Shwetank
Mukherjee, Poulami
Singh, Prabhakar
Kumari, Archana
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: A water-soluble, simple, and facile nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were developed by a single-step via the pyrolysis process and utilized in the fluorometric detection of dopamine (DA). DA is a neurotransmitter and plays a vital role in regulating several biological activities in mammalians. DA can oxidize into DA-quinone by the addition of N-CDs. The emission intensity of N-CDs can be quenched by DA through the inner filter effect (IFE) procedure. This detection mechanism is characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime decay study. The synthesized N-CDs are highly selective and sensitive towards DA, and their detection limit is 0.2 nM with a linear range of 0.001−1 μM. More importantly, N-CDs are utilizing to detect DA in the human blood serum. The as-synthesized N-CDs have excellent optical properties and are potentially used as a staining material for multicolor cellular imaging of MDA-MB 468 human breast cancer cells. Moreover, N-CDs are also used as a fluorescent ink and can be used for information storage. Hence, the as-synthesized N-CDs are explored as fluorescence materials for DA sensing, multicolor cellular imaging, and fluorescent ink.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DA sensing
Fluorescent ink
IFE
N-CDs
Multicolor cellular imaging
Real sample analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: XRD and Raman spectra of N-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, India .
This work was financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs obtained at various excitation wavelengths (350-480 nm) with an increment of 10 nm, (b) normalized emission spectra of N-CDs, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, DA, and DA-quinine [inset photographic image of N-CDs in day light, UV-light (365 nm), and after the addition of DA under UV-light], and (d) absorption spectra of the oxidized form of DA (DA-quinone) and DA in the wavelength range (400-700 nm).
(a) FTIR & (b) XPS spectral analysis of N-CDs and high-resolution XPS spectra study of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, and (e) O 1s, respectively.
(a) TEM image, (b) particle size distribution histogram, (c) HRTEM image shows interfringes distance, and (d) SAED pattern of as-synthesized N-CDs.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence bar diagram of N-CDs by the addition of same amount (10 μM) of various analytes [glucose (Glu), Fructose (Fru), ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), dopamine (DA), epinephrine (EP), norepinephrine (NEP), uric acid (UA), aspartic acid (As A), cysteine (Cys), arginine (Arg), and serine (Ser)], (b) fluorescence bar diagram of N-CDs in by the addition of interfering biomolecules, (c) fluorescence quenching spectra of N-CDs by the addition of various amounts of DA (0-10 μM), and (d) Stern-Volmer calibration curve with different concentration of DA [in inset linear relationship between the concentration of DA and emission intensities].
(a) Fluorometric sensing of DA in human blood serum by the synthesized N-CDs and (b) linear relationship between the concentrations of DA and emission intensities of N-CDs.
(a) The spectral overlap between absorption spectrum of DA-quinone and fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs during the IFE process and (b) TCSPC analysis of N-CDs and N-CDs + DA.

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra [inset Fig. 8(a-I) the fluorescent images under UV-light (365 nm)] of N-CDs with various concentration of DA (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 μM), and inset Fig. 8(a-II) shows fluorescent ink application of N-CDs by the irradiation of UV light (365 nm) and (b) cytotoxicity analysis of the as-prepared N-CDs at several concentrations [0-100 μg/mL].
In vitro fluorescent microscopic cellular images of MDA-MB 468 cells by the N-CDs at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm [blue], 480 nm [green], 550 nm [red], and merge fields, respectively.
Schematic diagram and working principle of the synthesized N-CDs.
Sensing mechanism for DA sensing by N-CDs.
The comparison between purposed materials with other reported materials for the determination of DA by the fluorometric method.
Sensing results of DA in 20 % human blood serum samples.

head.ce:title: Single-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots and applied for dopamine sensing, in vitro multicolor cellular imaging as well as fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, 826004, Jharkhand, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad-826004, Jharkhand, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, S. B. College, Ara (V.K.S.U.), Bihar, 802301, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, S. B. college, Ara (V.K.S.U.), Bihar-802301, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A water-soluble, simple, and facile nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were developed by a single-step via the pyrolysis process and utilized in the fluorometric detection of dopamine (DA). DA is a neurotransmitter and plays a vital role in regulating several biological activities in mammalians. DA can oxidize into DA-quinone by the addition of N-CDs. The emission intensity of N-CDs can be quenched by DA through the inner filter effect (IFE) procedure. This detection mechanism is characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime decay study. The synthesized N-CDs are highly selective and sensitive towards DA, and their detection limit is 0.2 nM with a linear range of 0.001−1 μM. More importantly, N-CDs are utilizing to detect DA in the human blood serum. The as-synthesized N-CDs have excellent optical properties and are potentially used as a staining material for multicolor cellular imaging of MDA-MB 468 human breast cancer cells. Moreover, N-CDs are also used as a fluorescent ink and can be used for information storage. Hence, the as-synthesized N-CDs are explored as fluorescence materials for DA sensing, multicolor cellular imaging, and fluorescent ink.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent materials with outstanding properties such as high photostability, excellent water solubility, easy functionality, and a higher degree of biocompatibility [1–3]. As reported in the previous literature, characteristically, the fluorescence properties of carbon dots depend on the presence of the functional groups and particle size [4–6]. Recently, researchers have focused on improving CDs by controlling size and modification [7,8]. It is still a challenge to prepare effective and secure methods to manage the fluorescence properties of CDs. To date, many ways have been reported to improve CDs’ photophysical properties, like doping and surface-passivation [4–8]. Doping is an additional factor to affect the optical properties of CDs [6–10]. After doping with different metals [9–12] and non-metals dopants [13–15], the optical and electronic properties of CDs will be changed [9,14,15]. The metal-doping into CDs core improves their chemical and physical properties, including quantum yield, fluorescent lifetime, electronic bandgap, etc. [16–19]. Numerous researchers have been synthesized metal-doped CDs and utilized in various fields, including sensing, imaging, catalysis, and drug delivery [9–12]. But many researchers claim that the metal doping was shown a toxic effect compared to non-metal doping [13–15]. So, non-metal doping is beneficial due to non-toxicity and simple synthesis procedures [17,17,18,19]. The non-metal doping into CDs core does not directly originate the fluorescence but significantly improves the electronic and optical properties of CDs [17,17,18,19]. The photoluminescence ability of CDs is one of the most important properties, but the exact mechanism of fluorescence from CDs has not been clearly defined. The researchers have proposed the fluorescence properties of CDs are depend on different factors, like the quantum confinement effect, the electronic transition from sp2 carbon-core, excitation-recombination radiation, and stabilizing surface trap [20–23]. Non-metal doping into CDs-core can affect the overall electronic distribution and energy level of CDs [24–26]. So, making a more effective radiative recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes can alter the optical properties of CDs [20–23]. On the other hand, non-metal doping into the carbon-core can alter the band gaps of CDs by strong coupling interaction with surface groups, like carboxyl, carbonyl, amine group, etc. [20–23]. Nowadays, various non-metal dopants such as S, N, B, and P are continuously used to monitor the optical properties of CDs [13–15,17,17,18,19]. Particularly, nitrogen-containing compounds passivized on the surface of CDs to improve their optical and chemical properties because nitrogen-containing groups increased the electron density inside the CDs [27–29].

Dopamine (DA) is an essential catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain, regulating many biological processes in the central nervous system [29–32]. The concentration of endogenous DA is 0.01−1 μM in the human brain [29–32]. The abnormal concentrations of DA in biological fluids can serve neurological disorders, like Parkinson, Schizophrenia, Huntington’s, renal, and cardiovascular diseases [33–36]. Therefore, selective and sensitive detection of DA is significant for diagnosing and monitoring these diseases. To date, many materials and methods have been reported to detect DA. For example, Tao et al. fabricated BSA-stabilized Au-nanoclusters for dopamine sensing via colorimetric and fluorometric methods [37]. Fabre et al. have established boronic acid-functionalized materials for the detection of dopamine via the electrochemical process [38]. Qian et al. have synthesized polymer-based fluorescence nanoparticles for dopamine sensing and imaging in living cells [39]. Fu et al. synthesized MOFs for the detection of dopamine through the electrochemiluminescence via resonance energy transfer [40]. Jiang et al. fabricated oxaborole-based polymer for the rapid sensing of dopamine via surface plasmon resonance process [41]. Among all the above-given procedures, the electrochemical technique has gained significant attention for many years because of its high sensitivity and low-cost [38]. But, interference from other biomolecules like ascorbic acid, epinephrine, norepinephrine, and uric acid limited their selectivity. Since the oxidation potential of ascorbic acid and uric acid is near DA, their concentration levels are 100–1000 times higher than that of DA [37–41]. So, the overlapping of the oxidation potential of these biomolecules in the electrochemical technique limits their applications [41–45]. The fluorometric technique overcomes these difficulties because of its simplified optical setup, high sensitivity, cheap, and good reproducibility [46–50]. In recent years, numerous scientists have paid more attention to the detection of DA by the fluorometric process [39,46–48]. However, a low concentration of DA in the complex biological medium, a new fluorescent probe for sensing of DA with high selectivity and sensitivity in the occurrence of interfering biomolecules is needed.

In this study, we have prepared N-CDs through a single-step and facile route for selective and sensitive sensing of DA. By the addition of DA solution into the N-CDs solution, the emission intensity of N-CDs was successfully quenched by the inner filter effect (IFE) process. DA could be oxidized into DA-quinone in an alkaline medium or ambient oxygen, and DA-quinone acts as an absorber [46–48], which causes quenching in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. The developed fluorescence sensor is simple, low-cost, and highly selective to DA with a linear range of 0.001−1 μM, and the detection of limit is 0.2 nM. We performed the DA sensing experiment under optimized conditions and demonstrated that this fluorescence sensor was highly selective to DA even in interfering biomolecules. Additionally, the as-prepared N-CDs are explored as a fluorescence probe for selective and sensitive sensing of DA in real samples. Moreover, high quantum yield and outstanding biocompatibility of N-CDs have been potentially used in multicolor cellular imaging of MDA-MB 468 human breast cancer cells and fluorescent ink. The tunable fluorescence properties of N-CDs have great potential applications in DA sensing, multicolor bioimaging, and fluorescent ink.

The N-CDs were synthesized by an easy calcination process in a solid phase [51,52]. Typically, 100 mg of citric acid and 10 mg of histidine (ratio 10:1) was taken and calcinated at 160 °C for 1 h in a hot air oven. Afterward, the black product was collected and grinded finely. Then the finely grinded particles were dissolved in 20 mL of purified water. The mixture solutions were sonicated for 2 min. to ensure more solubility of prepared N-CDs. Then the suspension was filtered using waterman filter paper to avoid large particles. The final solution obtained exhibits yellowish-brown and bluish color under normal light and UV-radiation (365 nm), respectively, as shown in Scheme 1 .

Fluorescence experiments were carried out in a 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell. Freshly prepared N-CDs stock solutions were diluted with PBS to give final concentration (10 μg/mL). Then, 2 mL (10 μg/mL) of N-CDs solution was added into 1 mL (10 μL/mL) solution of different analytes (glucose, fructose, ascorbic acid, citric acid, uric acid, DA, epinephrine, norepinephrine, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and Na+). After stirring for 20 min, the emission spectra were analyzed at 370 nm (excitation wavelength). Here, fluorescence intensity is significantly quenched by epinephrine, norepinephrine, and DA, while the other analytes have little effect.

For the cytotoxicity study of N-CDs on MDA-MB 468 cells was analyzed by MTT assay. The cells were planted with a 96-well microplate in the density of 3000 cells/well and incubated for the whole night. The cultured medium was discarded, and the cells were incubated by different concentrations of N-CDs (0−100 μg/mL) for 24 h. Afterward, discarding the cultured medium, 200 μL of MTT solution was added to each well and incubated for the next 4 h (37 °C). The absorption value of formazan crystal was examined at 570 nm at a microplate reader. Each sample replicates four times by the MTT assay.

For in vitro cell imaging analysis of N-CDs, MDA-MB 468 cells were cultivated in DMEM and added streptomycin (100 μg/mL), penicillin (50 μg/mL), and fetal bovine serum (10 %). The cells were planted in a 35 mm plate with cell density (3000 cells/well) and incubated for 24 h in a humidified incubator having 5 % CO2 (at 37 °C). Subsequently, the cells were washed with DMEM twice, and cell imaging experiments were performed on fluorescent microscopy.

The functionality of the precursor materials and the surface passivation of the N-CDs were detected by Thermo Nicolet Nexus FTIR (model 870). The particle size and shape of N-CDs were analyzed by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL 3010, Japan). The optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs were determined by Perkin Elmer LS 55 Fluorescence and Perkin-Elmer 950 spectrometer. The crystallinity properties of N-CDs were analyzed by the X-ray diffractometer (Phillips PW 1710 with Cu-Kα radiation). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) ESCALAB 250 spectrometer determined the elemental compositions and binding energy of N-CDs. The fluorescence lifetime analyzed by time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments. The multicolor cellular imaging of MDA-MB 468 cells was performed using a fluorescence microscope (Hitachi 7400). The cytotoxicity study of N-CDs was examined by an H1 ELISA plate reader (BioTek, USA).

The human blood serum samples were collected from the health center, IIT(ISM) DHANBAD. Firstly, the protein was removed from blood serum by the previously reported method [53,54]. The human blood serum (2 mL) was added into HClO4 acid (10 mL). The mixture solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm to minimize nonspecific protein interactions. The obtained product was spiked with the different concentrations of DA and examined by the given procedure.

The optical properties of the as-prepared N-CDs were analyzed by the photoluminescence and UV–vis spectroscopy study. We explored the fluorescence properties of N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths from 350−480 nm by an increment of 10 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The obtained result shows that the emission peak of N-CDs is excitation dependent; the maximum emission is shown at 455 nm when the excitation wavelength is 370 nm. The emission bands are red-shifted from 450−550 nm under excitation by longer wavelengths (350−480 nm), as shown in Fig. 1(b). This shifting may be due to the quantum confinement effect, various particle sizes, and nature of the N-CDs surface, which narrowing the band-gap of the N-CDs [13–15]. Simultaneously, we investigate the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, DA, and N-CDs with DA are shown in Fig. 1(c). The obtained results demonstrate that the N-CDs display an absorption peak at 340 nm due to the n-π* electronic transition of CO and CN groups of N-CDs. DA shows a sharp peak at 280 nm, indicating typical absorption of π-orbital of the aromatic ring [29–32]. Afterward, by the addition of DA (10 μM) into N-CDs solution, DA leads to oxidizing into DA-quinone in slightly basic condition or ambient oxygen, and a new broad absorption band is generated at 480 nm [29–32] is shown in Fig. 1(d). However, no absorption band is observed in the wavelength of 300−800 nm for DA is also shown in Fig. 1(d). Additionally, the synthesized N-CDs exhibit yellowish-brown color in day-light and bright-bluish color in UV-light in the aqueous medium. Subsequently, with the addition of DA into the synthesized N-CDs, the bluish fluorescent color diminishes, which confirms that DA is bound with the N-CDs is shown in inset Fig. 1(c).

Furthermore, N-CDs were prepared at several temperatures (120−180 °C). The fluorescence spectra were recorded, and maximum intensity was recorded for at a synthesis temperature of 160 °C, as shown in SI-1(a). It may be due to maximum carbonization of the materials are occurred at this specific temperature. So, 160 °C is ideal for the preparation of N-CDs. Simultaneously, the fluorescence stability of N-CDs was analyzed under high ionic strength using the different concentrations (0.2–2.0 mol/L) of NaCl solutions. There is no significant change in the emission intensities of N-CDs is shown in SI-2(a). The obtained results show that the as-prepared N-CDs have great potential applications to use as a sensor under harsh environments and sophisticated conditions. Concurrently, we have analyzed the emission spectra of the synthesized N-CDs by adding of different buffer solutions (pH = 1.0–14.0), as shown in SI-2(b). The fluorescence intensities of N-CDs increase (pH1-pH7) and decreases at higher pH-value (pH8-pH14). The obtained results show that the emission properties of N-CDs strongly depend on the pH value. The emission spectra were recorded for the N-CDs solution in different solvents (DCM, DMSO, acetone, chloroform, THF, ethanol, and water). The maximum intensity peak was observed in water, as shown in SI-2(c). So, we have selected water as a solvent throughout the experiments. Likewise, the fluorescence stability properties of N-CDs are checked with respect to time (one day and one month). The obtained result shows that the emission intensities of N-CDs are stable for months, as shown in SI-2(d).

The FTIR spectrum analysis examined the functional groups present on the surface of the synthesized N-CDs is given in Fig. 2 (a). The broad absorption band appears at 3534−3442 cm−1 due to the NH/OH stretching vibration of N-CDs [37–39]. The absorption band appears at 2962 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 1715 cm−1 indicates the skeletal vibration of the heteroaromatic imidazole of N-CDs. This demonstrates that the formation of amide linkage on the N-CDs surface [46–50]. The absorption band ranges of 1410−1541 cm−1 corresponds to imidazole-4-carboxylic acid [51,52], and the band at 1080 cm−1 shows bending vibration of COH. The absorption band at 1020 cm-1 corresponds to CN stretching vibration, and the peak at 790 cm−1 is for COH deformation vibration. So, the FTIR spectrum confirms that the imidazole groups react with carboxylic groups of citric acid to form N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) successfully.

The XPS analysis determines the electronic state and chemical compositions of the N-CDs and is mainly composed of C 1s (283.38 eV), N 1s (398.7 eV), and O 1s (529.46 eV) is shown in Fig. 2(b). The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum shows three peaks 282.17 eV, 283.26 eV, and 284.9 eV, which resembles CC, CC/CH, and COOH/CN [48,49], correspondingly is given in Fig. 2(c). The N 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into two bands at 397.22 (imidazolic NH group) and 399.10 eV (NCO groups) [48,49] is given in Fig. 2(d). The O 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.21, 530.04, and 531.43 eV corresponding to CO, COH, and OCOH bond [31,32], as given in Fig. 2(e). XPS analysis also analyzed the elemental composition of N-CDs and composed of C (65.27 %), N (12.96 %), and O (21.77 %). These obtained results are associated with the FTIR result, as given in Fig. 2(a).

The particle morphology and size of the as-prepared N-CDs were established via the HRTEM examination. The obtained result demonstrates that N-CDs have proper spherical morphology with an average particle is 3.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The corresponding nanoparticle size distribution histogram indicates that these particles are well dispersed and a diameter ranging from 1.5 to 4.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(b). The HRTEM study shows that the graphene crystalline lattice structure of N-CDs with the inter-fringes distance of 0.22 nm is shown in Fig. 3(c). The diffused ring patterns in SAED confirmed that the synthesized N-CDs are amorphous in nature [19–22], as shown in Fig. 3(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD and Raman spectra of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Phase purity, degree of crystallinity, and physical properties have been explored by the powder XRD analysis. The XRD pattern of N-CDs displays a slightly sharp and intense diffraction band about 2θ = 23.0, which corresponds to (002) plane [31,32] is shown in Fig. 4 (a). This plane (002) displays that the synthesized N-CDs are amorphous in nature, which may be ascribed to the close packing of CC, CO, CC, and CN groups [55,56]. The Raman spectrum of the N-CDs illustrates a disordered band (D) and a crystalline band (G) at 1349 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1, respectively is Fig. 4(b). It is observed that the intensity ratio (ID/IG) of N-CDs is 1.07, which is comparable with the common graphene material (1.05) [26–28]. This indicates that the intercalation of N-atom conjugation with the carbon backbone has led to somewhat disorder structures.

The fluorescence quenching analysis was carried out in the presence of different analytes (glucose, fructose, ascorbic acid, citric acid, uric acid, DA, epinephrine, norepinephrine, aspartic acid, cysteine, arginine, serine, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and Na+). The histograms are plotted with an emission intensity of N-CDs versus concentration (10 μM) of different analytes at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm are given in Fig. 5 (a). The plot demonstrates that the emission intensity is significantly quenched by epinephrine, norepinephrine, and DA whiles the little effect of other analytes is shown in Fig. 5(a). The similar structure of epinephrine and norepinephrine cause fluorescence quenching; however, final fluorescence intensities are higher than that of DA. The region behind this the steric hindrance caused by 2-(methylamino) ethyl group of epinephrine and 2-aminohydroxy ethyl of norepinephrine, which decreases the adsorption on the surface of N-CDs. The selectivity assay of N-CDs was checked in the existence of possible interfering analytes (glucose, fructose, ascorbic acid, epinephrine, norepinephrine, citric acid, uric acid, aspartic acid, cysteine, arginine, and serine), as shown in Fig. 5(b). The same amounts (10 μM) of interfering analytes were added into N-CDs solution; there is no significant variation in the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs. Subsequently, with the addition of DA (10 μM) solution into the interfering biomolecules and N-CDs solution, the emission intensity quenches. However, in the presence of epinephrine and norepinephrine do not cause interference. Because, these concentrations in blood serum are less than the 300 pg/mL, and the concentration of DA is 10–100 times higher, the influence of epinephrine and norepinephrine are insignificant [37–41]. The result shows that the quenching effect of DA is not affected even in the presence of interfering biomolecules. So, this technique is highly selective and specific to DA, as shown in Scheme 2 .

The fluorescence quenching spectra were examined by the addition of various concentrations (0−10 μM) of DA solution into N-CDs solution is shown in Fig. 5(c). N-CDs’ fluorescence intensity continuously reduces with increasing DA concentration, and shifting towards higher wavelengths from 452 to 462 nm was observed, as shown in Fig. 5(c). The possible region behind this quenching, DA can oxidize into DA-quinone by adding N-CDs solution and adsorb on the surface of N-CDs by the intermolecular H-bonding or electrostatic interaction [46–48]. The fluorescence quenching performance of N-CDs to DA is intensely associated with the imidazole ring of N-CDs and the catechol group of DA. DA molecules co-exist with phenoxy anions and H+ ions in the aqueous solution [29–31]. Phenoxy anions are labile and adsorb on the surface of N-CDs due to donor-acceptor-bridge structures [29–31]. The donor-acceptor-bridge structures of N-CDs with DA molecules increasing the π-conjugation and form a ground-state complex to quench the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs. This complex structure excludes the other interfering biomolecules, resulting in a better selectivity. The anchoring effect of imidazolic-nitrogen of N-CDs enhanced the sensitivity towards DA by the strong electron-donating group. The linear decreases in emission intensities of N-CDs with DA concentrations (0.001−1 μM), as given in [inset Fig. 5(d)]. This quenching shows a good linear relationship [F0/F = 204.77 + 5.21 × C DA (10 μM)] with a correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.995), and detection limit is 0.2 nM (3 x SD/K). This result is comparable with earlier projected approaches for DA sensing, as given in Table 1 .

The established method was used to detect a trace amount of DA in the human blood serum. The blood sample was spiked with known amounts of DA are shown in Table 2 . The fluorescence intensities regularly decrease with increasing the DA concentrations, as revealed in Fig. 6 (a). The fluorescence intensities and concentration of DA exhibited a good linear response [F0/F = 10.77 + 2.14 × C DA (20 nM)] in the range of 0−20 nM with a correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.998), as shown in Fig. 6(b). The detection limit was estimated to be 0.46 nM, which is significantly lower than the DA found in the human blood serum (0.01−1 μM). So, the results are shown in Table 2 indicates that the recovery of DA by standard addition technique is found to be 99.57–102.8 %, illustrate that the present work for DA detection is accurate, analogous, and satisfactory for the real samples study.

We have examined the probable detection mechanism behind the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by DA. Here, two probable mechanisms to detection DA by the synthesized N-CDs, either IFE or FRET. There should be a significant spectral overlap between fluorophore (N-CDs) emission intensity and the quencher’s absorption band (DA-quinone). Here, the broad range of emission spectrum of N-CDs (400–550 nm) should significant spectral overlap with the absorption band (450–540 nm) of DA-quinone, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). The possible mechanism was investigated by a single-photon counting experiment at an excitation wavelength (370 nm) is given in Fig. 7(b). The N-CDs’ fluorescence lifetime is 13.7 ns, after the addition of DA; the lifetime value remains constant 13.6 ns, which suggests static quenching. Because of in the static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophore remains constant [32]. Hence, the quenching of the emission intensity of N-CDs by DA by the IFE process, not the FRET method. In the IFE mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime value of fluorophore remains constant [32].

To prove fluorescence spectra and fluorescent images confirmed the ability of the projected method for the visual detection of DA by adding several amounts of DA in the solution. Fig. 8 (a) & (a–I) are shows that the emission intensities and brightness [under UV-light (365 nm)] of N-CDs regularly decreases with increasing the DA concentrations (0−10 μM). The obtained results suggested that DA’s addition can successfully reduce the blue fluorescent color of N-CDs under UV-light (365 nm). So, these N-CDs could be used as a fluorescence probe for the naked-eye detection of DA in real samples.

The outstanding optical properties of the carbon dots have been used in various fields. Beyond the fluorescence sensing of DA, the as-synthesized N-CDs are used as a fluorescent Ink [59,60]. The excellent optical properties of fluorescent ink, it can be used in information storage. Some alphabets are written on filter paper for fluorescent ink applications, and their image was taken under UV-light (365 nm). The bright-blue fluorescent color was displayed by UV-light irradiation, as shown in inset Fig. 8 (a-II). The written alphabets have outstanding fluorescence stability after storage of several months at ambient temperature. Therefore, the synthesized N-CDs are transparent, everlasting, and eco-friendly and can be utilized to fluorescence probe in our daily lives for several applications, including fluorescent ink and fingerprinting, without any modifications.

The MTT examination was used to analyze the cell-cytotoxicity activity of N-CDs towards MDA-MB 468 cells. The concentration of N-CDs was 10 μg/mL; the cell feasibility is around 99 %. When the concentrations of N-CDs were increased from 10−100 μg/mL, no significant change occurred in the cell viability of N-CDs after incubated at 24 h is given in Fig. 8(b). Thus, the N-CDs are exciting candidates for biomedical applications.

To estimate the cell imaging experiments of N-CDs towards MDA-MB 468 cells is shown in Fig. 9 . The MDA-MB 468 cells are incubated with N-CDs exhibits blue, green, red, and merge fields’ fluorescence under excitation of 330−550 nm. The close observation demonstrates that the N-CDs distributed over the cytoplasm, cell membrane, and the cell nucleus. Thus, the result shows that the as-prepared N-CDs have low-cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility and are suitable for cell imaging experiments.

In the current work, we have recognized a label-free fluorescence turn-off approach for the selective and sensitive sensing of DA. The aqueous solution of N-CDs emits robust blue fluorescent colors by irradiation of UV-light (365 nm) and high quantum yield (31.4 %). The addition of DA has successfully quenched the fluorescence intensity of synthesized N-CDs. The probable sensing mechanism for DA sensing was illustrated by lifetime decay and UV–vis spectroscopy analysis. The N-CDs show good aqueous solubility, outstanding biocompatibility, brilliant photophysical properties, and inertness to interfere with different analytes. The as-prepared N-CDs show the outstanding result in the visual detection of DA. The brilliant optical properties of the as-synthesized N-CDs have been potentially utilized as a staining probe for multicolor cellular imaging of MDA-MB 468 human breast cancer cells. Moreover, N-CDs were also used in fluorescent ink for writing, which enlightens rapidly as a bright-blue fluorescent color under UV-light (365 nm) without any further treatment. Consequently, N-CDs can be an outstanding fluorescence probe for DA sensing, multicolor cellular imaging, and fluorescent ink.

Amit Kumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Shwetank Asu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Poulami Mukherjee: Writing - review & editing. Prabhakar Singh: Investigation, Resources, Formal analysis. Archana Kumari: Writing - review & editing. Sumanta Kumar Sahu: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.113019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: 1. Chemicals and Reagents; 2. Quantum Yield Calculation; S-1: (a) Quantum yield calculation graph of the synthesized N-CDs and (b) N-CDs synthesized at different temperatures (120–180 °C); S-2: (a) the effect of different salt solutions (0.2–2.0 mol/L), (b) effect of different pH, (c) effect of different solvents on the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, and (d) fluorescence stability of the synthesized N-CDs.
The following is Supplementary data to this article:
